headings.title: File naming for prompts, responses, and wrappers

sections.title: Work Folder — Agent Instructions
sections.text:
# Work Folder — Agent Instructions

This folder manages planning, task packaging, and git worktrees for the
RFQ Automation platform. The agent working here does NOT implement code
or run tests — it organizes planning documents, creates task folders for
external models, manages worktree rebases, and maintains project state.

sections.title: Cross-session work log — READ FIRST
sections.text:
## Cross-session work log — READ FIRST

`~/work/rfqautomation-linux/WORK_LOG.md` (gitignored) holds cross-session
context that used to live in the disabled memory tool: user feedback to
apply going forward, decisions on open questions, pointers to active
initiatives, and durable how-tos for tooling. Read it at the start of
every session and append new entries on top as they come up.

sections.title: Specialist Agents
sections.text:
## Specialist Agents

Procedural knowledge lives in `~/work/agents/*.md`. Read the agent file
and pass its content as the prompt when spawning a sub-agent via the
Claude Code Agent tool. The sub-agent gets full procedure in-context
without needing to read external files.

sections.title: Routing Table
sections.text:
### Routing Table

| Cue | Agent | Inputs | Mode |
|-----|-------|--------|------|
| E2E tests need triggering, failing, screenshots, local repro | `agents/e2e-operator.md` | worktree_path, branch, task, scenario? | Tool-enabled |
| QA: unit tests, report regeneration, Gemini visual QA, schema verification | `agents/qa-operator.md` | task, worktree_path or artifact_path, run_id?, focus? | Tool-enabled |
| Create/list/sync/remove worktrees | `agents/worktree-operator.md` | task, name, base_branch? | Tool-enabled |
| Rebase, squash, multi-parent deps, integration branches, jj | `agents/jj-operator.md` | task, branch, target?, parents? | Tool-enabled |
| Reorganize PR commits: split/reorder/rewrite for review readability, per-commit test verification, force-push | `agents/commit-hygiene-operator.md` | mode (`reorganize` or `audit`), branch, base, target_commit_plan? | Tool-enabled |
| Audit/edit AGENTS.md and the agents/ ecosystem against architecture rules (procedures live in operators; AGENTS.md is routing+topology only) | `agents/agentsmd-curator.md` | mode (`audit` / `edit` / `add-operator`), agentsmd_path, agents_dir, findings_to_fix? | Tool-enabled |
| Read/comment/transition JIRA issues on lamaai.atlassian.net (e.g., INFA-34); auth via $JIRA_API_KEY + aaron.solomon@scint.ai | `agents/jira-operator.md` | task (`read` / `comment` / `transition` / `search` / `create`), issue_key, body? / target_status? / jql? | Tool-enabled |
| AGENTS.md maintenance workflow: inventory→audit→triage→plan→risk-gate→apply→verify; dispatches curator + risk sub-agents | `agents/agentsmd-maintenance-orchestrator.md` | agentsmd_path, agents_dir, triage_policy?, risk_gate_required? | Tool-enabled |
| Run CodeRabbit review loop on a branch; iterate until value drops to zero (3-6 passes typical); never push during loop | `agents/coderabbit-operator.md` | branch, base, worktree-path, test-command?, max-passes? | Tool-enabled |
| Verify a workflow execution complied with its operator procedure (read-only, post-execution review of step logs) | `agents/workflow-reviewer.md` | operator_file, step_log, expected_outputs?, mode? | Tool-enabled |
| Manage pipeline scratch artifact naming + .gitignore in a worktree (setup / audit / cleanup) | `agents/pipeline-artifacts-operator.md` | worktree-path, mode | Tool-enabled |
| Package task folder for external model | `agents/task-packager.md` | task_name, task_type, paths | Tool-enabled |
| Review PR: risk assessment, research, decomposition, post comments | `agents/pr-review-operator.md` | pr_number, repo?, project_dir? | Tool-enabled |
| Multi-round adversarial PR justification workflow: interrogator ↔ researcher ↔ value assessor ↔ adjudicator, per-thread drop/backlog/keep (used by pr-review-operator Phase 4b, never alone) | `agents/pr-justification-gauntlet.md` | pr_number, work_dir, repo?, project_dir?, planning_dir? | Tool-enabled |
| Sub-agents of the justification gauntlet (never invoked directly by humans): conservative interrogator, evidence researcher, value assessor, per-round adjudicator | `agents/pr-justification-interrogator.md`, `agents/pr-justification-researcher.md`, `agents/pr-justification-value-assessor.md`, `agents/pr-justification-adjudicator.md` | thread state from the gauntlet | Tool-enabled |
| Secondary FastAPI review: framework idioms, service/controller/repo architecture, state+concurrency, Pydantic contracts, testing+observability (runs after primary passes) | `agents/fastapi-review-operator.md` | pr_number, repo?, project_dir? | Tool-enabled |
| Version bump, Docker/Windows build, promote, dry-run | `agents/release-operator.md` | task, version? | Tool-enabled |
| Measure coverage, map gaps, find dead/low-value tests | `agents/coverage-analyzer.md` | task, worktree_path, scope? | Tool-enabled |
| Investigate intended behavior from commits/tickets/goals | `agents/behavior-investigator.md` | target, worktree_path, context? | Tool-enabled |
| Assess risk + value of uncovered code, priority ranking | `agents/risk-assessor.md` | uncovered_areas, worktree_path, coverage_data? | Tool-enabled |
| Record Playwright traces for ambiguous behaviors, human review docs | `agents/trace-recorder.md` | workflow, worktree_path, app_url, ambiguity, questions | Tool-enabled |
| Write tests for VERIFIED intended behavior only | `agents/test-writer.md` | behavior_spec, worktree_path, test_type, target | Tool-enabled |
| Audit existing tests: flag unverified captures, dead tests, low-value | `agents/coverage-auditor.md` | task, worktree_path, test_files?, behavior_specs? | Tool-enabled |

sections.title: How to Invoke
sections.text:
### How to Invoke

1. Read the agent file: `Read ~/work/agents/<agent>.md`
2. Construct a prompt combining the agent file content + dynamic context (branch names, paths, specific task)
3. Spawn via Agent tool with the combined prompt
4. The sub-agent has full procedure knowledge and tool access — it handles the rest

sections.title: When NOT to Delegate
sections.text:
### When NOT to Delegate

- Quick single-command operations (e.g., `git worktree list`) — just run them directly
- Reading/inspecting files — use Read/Grep directly
- Ambiguous requests — clarify with the user first, then delegate

sections.title: uv/pip packaging migration
sections.text:
### uv/pip packaging migration
Replace PyInstaller + zip packaging with `uv sync --frozen` and a private PyPI registry on S3. Risk assessment: NOT PASSING (Audit MEDIUM, Scope HIGH, Shortcut HIGH). Blocked on 5 design documents:
1. Offline/cache-seeding strategy for air-gapped on-prem customers
2. Torch GPU/CPU variant selection via uv extra indexes
3. Windows service model without PyInstaller (pythonw.exe? nssm? embedded Python distribution?)
4. Third-party package mirroring strategy (full mirror vs selective vs proxy vs managed service)
5. Supply chain security: vulnerability scanning, automated patching, package integrity (Dependabot, Trivy, Renovate, Sigstore)

After those are resolved, re-scope milestone 1 as: private registry + one internal package published + one end-to-end install proof-of-concept.

sections.title: Folder Structure
sections.text:
## Folder Structure

```
~/work/
  rfqautomation-linux/          # App repo — on main (never commit here directly)
  rfqinstallation-linux/        # Installation/setup repo
  agents/                       # Specialist agent files
  worktrees/                    # Git worktrees — one per active task
  planning/
    e2e/                        # E2E testing project
      tasks/                    #   Task definitions (00-22)
      execution-order.md        #   Dependency graph, phases
      OVERVIEW.md               #   Executive overview
      decisions.md              #   Decision log (D1-D25)
    cloud/                      # Cloud deployment (Railway + Supabase, NOT AWS)
      complete-stack-architecture.md
      executive-roadmap.md
      roadmap-mvp.md            #   MVP for 10 users
    design/                     # Risk analysis and design docs
    distribution/               # License server + patch server initiative
    product/                    # Product enhancements
      expansion/                #   Product expansion roadmap (tasks 00-18)
  Startup Package/              # App seed data (Excel files)
  e2e-local-settings.zip        # Gitignored secrets for E2E tests
```

sections.title: Branch Flow
sections.text:
## Branch Flow

```
feat/<name> (worktree) → draft PR → review → main
```

- **`main`** — protected. All work merges here via reviewed PRs.
- **`feat/<name>`** — feature branches created from `main` in worktrees.
- **`basis/<name>`** — multi-parent basis branches (managed by jj-operator).
- **`integration/<name>`** — temporary integration branches for cross-PR testing.
- **All PRs opened in draft mode.**

sections.title: Project branch documentation
sections.text:
### Project branch documentation

When a project spans multiple PRs with dependencies, maintain a tracking
file in `planning/` that maps:

```markdown
## Branch Map — <Project Name>

| Branch | PR | Parents | Status |
|--------|-----|---------|--------|
| feat/phase-1 | #NNN | main | draft |
| feat/phase-2 | #NNN | main | draft |
| basis/phase-3-deps | — | feat/phase-1 + feat/phase-2 | local |
| feat/phase-3 | #NNN | basis/phase-3-deps | draft |
| integration/project | — | all of above | testing |
```

sections.title: Running External Models
sections.text:
## Running External Models

The `agents` CLI (`~/.local/bin/agents`) runs external models and agents.

```bash
# Run a model directly (no agent file needed):
agents -m gpt-high -p /home/nes/work/rfqautomation-linux "your prompt here"
agents -m claude-opus -p /home/nes/work/rfqautomation-linux "your prompt here"

# Run from a prompt file:
agents -m gpt-high -p /home/nes/work/rfqautomation-linux -f prompt.md

# Run a named agent (from agents/ directory):
agents my-agent "prompt args"

# Available models: gpt-high, gpt-medium, gpt-low, gpt-xhigh,
#   claude-opus, claude-sonnet, claude-haiku,
#   gemini-high, gemini-pro-high, gemini-flash-high
# Config: ~/.config/oulipoly-agent-runner/models/
```

Models have tool access (file read/write, bash, search) and operate on the
`-p` project directory. They run to completion and print results to stdout.

sections.title: Implementation & Bug-Fix Workflow
sections.text:
## Implementation & Bug-Fix Workflow

All code changes follow the same pipeline. Bugs start with RCA; features skip it.

| Step | Model | Role |
|------|-------|------|
| 1. RCA (bugs only) | `gpt-high` | Investigate root cause. Read code, trace failure, produce report. Do NOT propose fixes. |
| 2. Proposal | `gpt-high` | Propose a fix/feature based on findings. Write a design proposal. Do NOT implement. |
| 3. Risk assessment | 3x `claude-opus` | Three sub-agents **in parallel**: audit risk + scope risk + shortcut risk. All must return **LOW**. If MEDIUM/HIGH, revise proposal and re-run. |
| 4. Research | `gpt-high` | **Only after all three risks are LOW.** Research hookpoints in the codebase. Understand what exists. Avoid building parallel systems. |
| 5. Test discovery | `agents/test-discovery.md` (`gpt-high`) | **After research, before implement.** Discover candidate governing tests for changed files. Output is a heuristic map — absence of a hit does NOT prove absence of a test; the implementer must verify before relying on it to skip new test work. A mechanical AST pass also enumerates touched public symbols (add/remove/rename) and flags any whose name has zero references in the candidate test set. Advisory — does not block. |
| 6. Red-phase gate | `agents/red-phase-gate.md` (`gpt-high` orchestrator) | **After test discovery, before implement.** Run newly-authored tests against HEAD and classify the red-phase state. Advisory — informs the implementer whether their tests target new behavior or mirror current behavior. Does not block. **Consumer: implementer only.** |
| 7. Implement | `gpt-high` | **Only after research completes.** Launch implementation sub-agents. |
| 8. Green-phase validation | `agents/green-phase-gate.md` (`gpt-high` orchestrator) | **After implement, before CodeRabbit.** Rerun the red-phase node IDs against the implemented HEAD. Classify each as turned-green, still-red, or regressed. Advisory — informs the implementer whether their implementation satisfies its own red-phase tests. Does not block. **Consumer: implementer only.** |
| 9. CodeRabbit review | CLI | **After green-phase validation.** Run `coderabbit review --plain --base main` in a loop: fix findings, `git commit --amend --no-edit`, rerun. Keep amending until CodeRabbit stops producing **valuable** findings — not until the report is empty. Design-preference churn and nitpicks that the prior pass already addressed are signals to stop. |
| 10. Test-audit gate | `agents/test-audit-gate.md` (`gpt-high` orchestrator) invoking 3x audit sub-agents in parallel | **After CodeRabbit converges, on the actual diff.** Run spec-alignment, test-quality, and coverage-delta audits in parallel. Both `FAIL` and `PARTIAL` block PR opening. `PASS` required in pr-review mode; in implementation mode, coverage-delta may be `PARTIAL` because there is no CI baseline pre-push — that specific `PARTIAL` does not block. |
| 11. Multi-concern review | `claude-opus` | **After step 10 records the audit evidence, on the actual diff.** Can this PR be decomposed into smaller single-concern PRs? Output is a split plan (one PR per concern, in dependency order) or an explicit "cannot decompose further". Runs against `git diff main..HEAD`, not the proposal. |
| 12. Justification review | `claude-opus` | **In parallel with step 11.** What deserves to be in this PR? What does not? Flags scope creep, incidental cleanups, and unrelated defects that should be their own PR. Together with steps 10 and 11 this is the final gate before opening PRs. |
| 13. Commit hygiene | `agents/commit-hygiene-operator.md` (`claude-opus`) | **After steps 10, 11, 12 agree on the split.** Audit each PR's commit sequence and reorganize if needed. Even single-PR diffs benefit from per-commit organization — a 600-line PR with one giant commit is not reviewable; the same 600 lines as 8 logical commits is. Operator handles split/reorder/message-rewrite + per-commit verification. |
| 14. Draft PR(s) | `gh` CLI | **After steps 10, 11, 12, and 13 agree.** Open one or more **draft** PRs against `main`. One PR per concern the reviewers isolated; dependency order. A single-concern change is one PR. |

**Principles:**
- Separate agents for separate concerns — RCA agents don't propose, proposal agents don't implement
- Zero-risk gate — all three risk assessments must be LOW before proceeding
- Shortcut detection — the shortcut risk assessment checks whether the proposal itself introduces hacks, duplicated logic, hardcoded values, source-vs-artifact confusion, or symptom-masking workarounds. A proposal that fixes one shortcut by introducing another fails this gate.
- **No compatibility shims** — never introduce backward-compatibility shims, feature flags for old behavior, or transitional code paths. Ship the change cleanly. Rely on thorough testing, not gradual rollout.
- Research before implementation — find hookpoints, avoid parallel systems
- Iterate until it passes — don't implement a proposal that hasn't passed risk assessment
- CodeRabbit stops when findings are valueless, not when the report is empty — track per-pass value (real architectural fixes vs design-preference churn) and stop when a pass returns only churn. Skip findings with documented rationale when needed; don't chase flip-flops
- Post-CodeRabbit review gates are the PR-opening gate — the test-audit, multi-concern, and justification reviews in steps 10, 11, and 12 run on the **actual diff**, not the proposal, because proposals can look clean while the diff bundles unrelated cleanups
- Commit hygiene is part of PR prep, not optional — a PR that survives steps 10+11+12 with bundled or unstructured commits still fails reviewability. Step 13 is the explicit gate that catches "the diff is right but the commits are noise"
- Draft-only — all PRs opened by this pipeline are drafts; promotion to "ready for review" is a human decision

sections.title: CodeRabbit Review Loop
sections.text:
## CodeRabbit Review Loop

CodeRabbit CLI is step 9 of the implementation pipeline. It iterates until per-pass value drops to zero (typically 3–6 passes), amending a single commit so each pass reviews a clean diff against `main`. The branch is never pushed during the loop.

→ see `agents/coderabbit-operator.md` for the pass procedure, convergence detection, pre-pass sanity checks, and rate-limit handling.

sections.title: Post-CodeRabbit Review Gates (steps 10 + 11 + 12)
sections.text:
## Post-CodeRabbit Review Gates (steps 10 + 11 + 12)

Once CodeRabbit converges, run three gates **in parallel** on the actual
diff before opening any PRs:

The **test-audit gate** runs `agents/test-audit-gate.md` on the actual diff
and synthesizes spec-alignment, test-quality, and coverage-delta audits.
Both `FAIL` and `PARTIAL` from the test-audit gate block PR opening, except
the implementation-mode coverage-delta `PARTIAL` which is acknowledged.

1. **Multi-concern review.** Can this PR be split into smaller PRs, one per
   concern, in dependency order? Reviewer outputs either a split plan
   (PR 1: X, PR 2: Y, PR 2 depends on PR 1) or "cannot decompose further".
2. **Justification review.** For every hunk in the diff, does it deserve to
   be in this PR? Reviewer flags scope creep, incidental cleanups, and
   unrelated defects that should ship separately.

All three gates run on `git diff main..HEAD`, not on the proposal or the RCA.
Proposals can look clean while the implementation diff bundles extra
concerns.

The orchestrator reconciles the three reports:

- If the test-audit gate returns `FAIL` or a blocking `PARTIAL` → do not
  open a PR yet; fix the blocking issue first.
- If the test-audit gate passes and both structural reviews say "single
  concern, minimal" → open one draft PR.
- If the test-audit gate passes and one or both structural reviews surface
  a split → open one draft PR per concern, in
  dependency order. Use `basis/<name>` branches when a later PR depends on
  an earlier one (see "Project branch documentation" above).
- If the structural reviews disagree, the more conservative split wins
  (prefer smaller PRs over larger ones).

sections.title: PR Review Workflow
sections.text:
## PR Review Workflow

After implementation and CodeRabbit, before merging, all PRs go through the
full review pipeline. **Use `agents/pr-review-operator.md` to run this.**

| Phase | Model | Role |
|-------|-------|------|
| 1. Risk assessment (3x parallel) | `claude-opus` | Audit risk + scope risk + shortcut risk. All must return **LOW**. |
| 2. Research verification | `gpt-high` | Verify the PR's technical approach against the codebase. Run in parallel with Phase 1. |
| 3. Test-audit gate | `agents/test-audit-gate.md` (`gpt-high` orchestrator) | Run spec-alignment, test-quality, and coverage-delta audits on the actual PR diff. `FAIL` and `PARTIAL` block the gate in PR review. |
| 4. Multi-concern check | `claude-opus` | Can this PR be decomposed into smaller PRs with single concerns? |
| 5. Justification check | `claude-opus` | What deserves to be in this PR? What does not? |
| 6. Commit hygiene check | `agents/commit-hygiene-operator.md` (`claude-opus`) in audit-only mode | Even if multi-concern says "cannot decompose further," are the PR's commits organized for incremental review? Operator runs in read-only audit mode here and posts findings; reorganization is the PR author's responsibility, not the reviewer's. |
| 7. Synthesize & post | Orchestrator | Post test-audit and risk-gate summaries, findings, decomposition, commit-hygiene observations, and questions as PR comments. |
| 8. Proposal loop (conditional) | `gpt-high` + 3x `claude-opus` | If the approach is fundamentally flawed, propose an alternative and risk-assess it. |
| 9. Domain-specific research (conditional) | `gpt-high` | Verify external-domain claims when the PR touches compliance, standards, or protocol-heavy behavior. |

**Rules:**
- Run on the actual diff, not the proposal — proposals can look reasonable while the diff bundles too much
- All three risk assessments must be LOW for the PR to pass the gate
- The test-audit gate runs in parallel with Phases 1 and 2, and `FAIL` / `PARTIAL` block PR approval in review mode
- A PR that touches N independent concerns should be split into N PRs
- Dependency order matters — if PR B depends on PR A, A merges first
- A large deletion (removing old code) is its own PR, separate from the addition that replaces it
- Additive changes (new params, new functions) go before behavioral changes (swapping the implementation)
- A PR that survives multi-concern + justification with messy commits still fails reviewability — request commit reorganization (see `agents/commit-hygiene-operator.md`)
- If the multi-concern check says "cannot decompose further" AND commits are organized, the PR is ready for merge
- All findings get posted to the PR — reviews are not just internal reports

sections.title: Workflow Step Reporting & Review
sections.text:
## Workflow Step Reporting & Review

Multi-step workflows must state each concrete action as it is performed (not summaries), and a separate reviewer agent verifies compliance against the operator's procedure after the workflow finishes.

→ see `agents/workflow-reviewer.md` for the step-reporting format, the when-to-review cases, and the review procedure.

sections.title: Running Sub-Agents
sections.text:
## Running Sub-Agents

Sub-agents are invoked via the `agents` binary (`~/.local/bin/agents`), not through Claude Code's built-in Agent tool. The binary is the CLI mode of [Oulipoly Agent Runner](https://github.com/nestharus/agent-runner).

sections.title: CLI Syntax
sections.text:
### CLI Syntax

```bash
agents [OPTIONS] [AGENT] [PROMPT...]
```

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-m, --model <MODEL>` | Execute a model directly (no agent file) |
| `-a, --agent-file <AGENT_FILE>` | Path to an agent `.md` file |
| `-f, --file <FILE>` | Read prompt from file |
| `-p, --project <PROJECT>` | Working directory |
| `-i, --input <KEY=VALUE>` | Pass model inputs as key=value (repeatable) |
| `--models-dir <MODELS_DIR>` | Override models directory |
| `--agents-dir <AGENTS_DIR>` | Override agents directory |

**Prompt resolution priority:** `--file` > positional arguments > stdin

sections.title: Common Patterns
sections.text:
### Common Patterns

```bash
# Model + prompt file (pipeline standard)
agents --model gpt-high --file prompt.md

# Agent file + model override
agents --agent-file ~/work/agents/e2e-operator.md --model claude-opus --file prompt.md

# Pipe prompt from stdin
cat spec.md | agents --model glm

# Set working directory
agents --model gpt-high -p /path/to/repo --file prompt.md
```

sections.title: Agent File Format
sections.text:
### Agent File Format

```markdown
---
description: 'One-line description'
model: claude-opus
output_format: ''
---

System prompt / instructions here.
```

sections.title: Input Schema
sections.text:
### Input Schema

Models can declare typed input parameters via `[[inputs]]` in their TOML configs. The runner validates user-supplied `-i key=value` flags against the schema and maps them to CLI flags on the wrapped command.

| Field | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | yes | Input identifier |
| `type` | yes | `string`, `integer`, `number`, `boolean`, `enum`, `array` |
| `flag` | no | CLI flag to pass to the command (e.g. `"--size"`) |
| `required` | no | Fail if not provided and no default |
| `default_input` | no | This is the unnamed positional input (the "prompt") |
| `default` | no | Default value when not provided by user |
| `description` | no | Human/AI-readable description |
| `options` | enum only | List of valid values |
| `min` / `max` | integer/number | Value range bounds |
| `item_type` | array only | Type of array elements |
| `min_items` / `max_items` | array only | Array length bounds |

**How inputs flow:**
- The `default_input` receives the positional prompt (args, `--file`, or stdin)
- Named inputs (`-i key=value`) are validated against the schema, then passed as `--flag value` to the command
- Repeated `-i` with the same key collects into an array (e.g. `-i images=a.png -i images=b.png`)
- Inputs with defaults are passed automatically when not overridden
- Unknown inputs pass through as `--key value`

**Stdout is raw bytes** — commands can output binary data (images, videos) and it passes through unmodified. Pipe to a file to save: `agents -m seedream-t2i "A cat" > cat.jpeg`

sections.title: Multimodal Models
sections.text:
### Multimodal Models

Image/video generation models use wrapper scripts as their `command`. The scripts handle HTTP APIs, polling, downloading — the runner just validates inputs and passes flags through.

```bash
# Generate an image (raw bytes on stdout, pipe to file)
agents -m seedream-t2i "A sunset over mountains" > sunset.jpeg

# Generate a video
agents -m seedance-t2v-low -i duration=5 -i resolution=480p "A whale swimming" > whale.mp4

# Image-to-video with source image
agents -m seedance-i2v-fast -i image=./photo.jpg "Slow camera orbit" > orbit.mp4

# Image editing with reference images
agents -m seedream-i2i -i image=input.png "Make it warmer" > edited.jpeg

# Chain: generate an image then animate it
agents -m seedream-t2i "A cat painting" > cat.jpeg
agents -m seedance-i2v-low -i image=cat.jpeg "The cat blinks slowly" > cat.mp4
```

sections.title: Running in Background
sections.text:
### Running in Background

Use the Claude Code Agent tool with `run_in_background: true`. Do NOT use `while pgrep` loops — you will be notified when the agent completes.

For file output from background bash commands, write the wrapper inside the worktree (see "File naming" below):
```bash
WT=~/work/worktrees/<name>
cat > "$WT/.run-agents.sh" << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
~/.local/bin/agents --model gpt-xhigh --file "$1" > "$2" 2>&1
EOF
chmod +x "$WT/.run-agents.sh" && "$WT/.run-agents.sh" "$WT/PROPOSAL.prompt.md" "$WT/PROPOSAL.md"
```

sections.title: File naming for prompts, responses, and wrappers
sections.text:
### File naming for prompts, responses, and wrappers

Pipeline scratch (prompts, responses, wrapper scripts, per-phase outputs) lives inside the worktree — never bare in `/tmp/` — to prevent cross-session filename collisions that have previously shipped bad work to background agents.

→ see `agents/pipeline-artifacts-operator.md` for the standard filename catalogue, `.gitignore` block, setup / audit / cleanup procedures, read-before-write policy, and background-task wrapper naming.

sections.title: Available Models
sections.text:
### Available Models

See `~/.config/oulipoly-agent-runner/models/` for the full list. Model selection guidance is in `src/models.md`.

sections.title: Adding Tasks
sections.text:
## Adding Tasks

| Type | Location | Naming |
|------|----------|--------|
| E2E | `planning/e2e/tasks/` | `NN-short-name.md` (next number) |
| Cloud | `planning/cloud/` | Update relevant file (no numbered tasks) |
| Product enhancement | `planning/product/` | `PRODUCT-enhancement-<name>.md` |
| Product expansion | `planning/product/expansion/tasks/` | `NN-short-name.md` |

Task numbers are stable — never reuse. Dropped tasks get `.DROPPED` suffix.

sections.title: Infrastructure Reference
sections.text: ## Infrastructure Reference

sections.title: JIRA
sections.text:
### JIRA

Two project boards, each team-managed (next-gen):

| Project | Key | Board | Scope | Issue types (board-visible) |
|---------|-----|-------|-------|-----------------------------|
| Infrastructure | `INFA` | [INFA board (#34)](https://lamaai.atlassian.net/jira/software/projects/INFA/boards/34) | CI/build, deployment, distribution, updater, release pipeline, infra tooling | `Task` (Epics are timeline/backlog only) |
| QA Testing | `KAN` | [KAN board (#1)](https://lamaai.atlassian.net/jira/software/projects/KAN/boards/1) | Frontend UX bugs, product bugs surfaced by QA or E2E, test-side fixes/harmonization, cross-scenario baseline drift | `Bug`, `Feature` |

**Routing rule of thumb:** if a ticket is about how the app *behaves from the user's perspective* or about E2E/QA tests themselves, file in `KAN`. If it is about how the app gets *built, shipped, or kept running* (CI, artifacts, updater, releases, deployment), file in `INFA`. When in doubt, file in the board the person reviewing it lives on.

**Auth:** Basic auth — `$AZURE_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_KEY`
**API base:** `https://lamaai.atlassian.net/rest/api/3`

```bash
# List all INFA issues
curl -s -u "$AZURE_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_KEY" \
  "https://lamaai.atlassian.net/rest/agile/1.0/board/34/issue?maxResults=50" \
  -H "Accept: application/json"

# List all KAN issues
curl -s -u "$AZURE_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_KEY" \
  "https://lamaai.atlassian.net/rest/agile/1.0/board/1/issue?maxResults=50" \
  -H "Accept: application/json"

# Create an INFA task
curl -s -u "$AZURE_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -X POST "https://lamaai.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/issue" \
  -d '{"fields":{"project":{"key":"INFA"},"summary":"...","issuetype":{"name":"Task"}}}'

# Create a KAN bug
curl -s -u "$AZURE_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -X POST "https://lamaai.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/issue" \
  -d '{"fields":{"project":{"key":"KAN"},"summary":"...","issuetype":{"name":"Bug"}}}'

# Attach a file to a KAN ticket (screenshots etc.)
curl -s -u "$AZURE_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_KEY" \
  -H "X-Atlassian-Token: no-check" \
  -X POST "https://lamaai.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/issue/KAN-NNN/attachments" \
  -F "file=@/path/to/screenshot.png"

# Add a comment (ADF format)
curl -s -u "$AZURE_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -X POST "https://lamaai.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/issue/<KEY>/comment" \
  -d '{"body":{"type":"doc","version":1,"content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"..."}]}]}}'

# Transition an issue (INFA: 11=To Do, 21=In Progress, 31=Done — KAN uses the same ids)
curl -s -u "$AZURE_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -X POST "https://lamaai.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/issue/<KEY>/transitions" \
  -d '{"transition":{"id":"21"}}'

# List transitions available from current status
curl -s -u "$AZURE_EMAIL:$JIRA_API_KEY" \
  "https://lamaai.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/issue/<KEY>/transitions" \
  -H "Accept: application/json"
```

**Link types (both projects):** `Blocks` (10000), `Cloners` (10001), `Duplicate` (10002), `Relates` (10003).

**Moving an issue between INFA and KAN:** the REST API does not expose cross-project move for team-managed projects. The supported workaround is:

1. Clone — POST `/issue` in the target project with the same `summary` + ADF `description` + appropriate `issuetype` (INFA → KAN maps `Task` → `Bug`, typically).
2. Link — POST `/issueLink` with `{"type":{"name":"Cloners"},"inwardIssue":{"key":"<new>"},"outwardIssue":{"key":"<old>"}}`.
3. Comment on the old issue pointing at the new key with a `<link>` mark in the ADF body.
4. Transition the old issue to `Done` (transition id `31`).

**PATCH via REST API for body/title updates:** `gh pr edit` and similar wrappers sometimes fail on this tenant with `Projects (classic) is being deprecated` GraphQL errors. Use `gh api -X PATCH /repos/<owner>/<repo>/pulls/<N> --input <json>` with `{"body": "..."}` to bypass.

sections.title: Railway API
sections.text:
### Railway API

**Token:** `RAILWAY_TOKEN` in shell env. Use GraphQL API, not Railway CLI.

**Endpoint:** `https://backboard.railway.app/graphql/v2`
**Auth:** `Authorization: Bearer $RAILWAY_TOKEN`

```bash
# List projects
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $RAILWAY_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"{ projects { edges { node { id name } } } }"}' \
  https://backboard.railway.app/graphql/v2
```

**Current projects:**
- `rfq/production` (`9dd09c0b-d01a-44f9-bb2f-a790fcba1d8f`) — empty
- `rfq/staging` (`d3b33915-f6ff-4b4a-8aad-ed4d865bde04`) — services: rfq-postgres, rfq-app, rfq-oauth
- `platform` (`9dd83dcc-578b-49d5-8ae0-31b5560357f8`) — empty

sections.title: AWS Access
sections.text:
### AWS Access

| Key | Env var prefix | Purpose |
|-----|---------------|---------|
| Customer app | `AWS_KEY` / `AWS_SECRET` | S3 for RFQ app (customer buckets) |
| Dev | `AWS_DEV_ACCESS_KEY_ID` / `AWS_DEV_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | Dev/CLI — IAM `aaron-solomon` (permanent) |
| GitHub | `AWS_GITHUB_ACCESS_KEY_ID` / `AWS_GITHUB_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | S3 promotion write (CI) |

Shell env has `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`/`AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` set to OpenTofu state backend. Use dev key with:
```bash
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="" AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="" aws s3 ls --profile default
```

**Account:** `629108221537` | **Region:** `us-east-1`

sections.title: Tailscale Network & Self-Hosted Runners
sections.text:

| `lamaai-dev` | `100.99.190.69` | Linux | Self-hosted GitHub Actions runner (lama-server) |

SSH: `sshpass -p "$LAMAMINI_PASSWORD" ssh rfq_user@<lamamini-ip>` or `sshpass -p "$LAMAAI_DEV_PASSWORD" ssh rfq_user@<lamaai-dev-ip>` (user `rfq_user`)

**LAMA_MINI runner setup:**
- WSL runner: `LAMA-MINI-WSL` — labels `self-hosted, linux, x64, e2e, wsl2, lama-mini` — active
- Windows runner: `LAMA_MINI` — labels `self-hosted, x64, windows` — active

sections.title: Global Constraints
sections.text:
## Global Constraints

- **`rfqautomation-linux/` stays on `main`** — all work in worktrees
- **All PRs opened in draft mode**
- **Cloud stack is Railway (compute) + Supabase (database)** — separate services, not AWS
- **MVP targets 10 users** — defer anything not needed at that scale
- **Never introduce required env vars for on-prem** — customers auto-update and don't modify `.env`. Hardcode defaults; env vars override, not define.
